### Post by Person98 on 2008-04-27
hey i would check the refresh rates of your monitors and make sure they are the appropriate ranges, you can find them on the manufacturers webstes. I had a similar problem because it couldn't auto detect one of them and that was how I fixed it. Also try adding: Option "DRI" "off" to your device section that was also giving me grief. Another pointer with 2 monitors is compositing windowmanagers will not work(for the most part) so make sure that desktop effects are disabled

---

### Post by phlancelot on 2008-05-30
Hey so I recently purchased a cheapo stleath radeon 9250 to slide into my last available PCI slot on my old-ish computer. My main video card is an AIW ATI radeon 9800 pro which has been working great for me for 3 years. 

I've come into possession of some extra LCD monitors so thought that I could venture spending 50bucks for a new card to get a dual monitor setup rocking (sadly my AIW pro is not dual head...)

Anyhow, I've got this all set up fine on windows in like 5 minutes flat. Now I dont want to rag on linux by saying this because I know i installed easy to use drivers that were made specifically for what i was doing within windows. 

I'm using ubuntu 8.04 hardy as my main OS though (sans games) and Id like to be able to get a dual monitor thing going on ubuntu as well.

So far I've tinkered a fair bit with Xorg.conf and my latest attempt at using this guide is still not giving me good results.

Basically I can get through all the steps and make a nice config file along the lines of what I see here but when I press CTRL-ALT-BCKSPC to test it out I seem to get several screen flashes before my main display goes into a very low resolution mode (like 800x600) and it gives me some error message about my display settings. The second monitor I have managed to power on at this time (when the primary one is flashing) but all I get is a big black screen with one line of white text at the top basically stating the name of my video card (the new stealth one that i just added) embedded in some other weird code text that I can't decipher.

I would type up exactly what it says but I can't seem to reproduce it right now. I'm also using an old xorg.conf file and I cant seem to find the one that I made in order to produce this weird set of errors.... I just thought I'd throw up the explanation of where I'm coming from first in case anyone has experience a similar set of issues and knows a quick fix for my problem.

I'm wondering primarily if I somehow dont have the Xinerama extension installed correctly... I do not recall ever installing this manually.... and some of the terminal commands that are in the various tutorials on the web don't seem to work (the lspci -X | g..... one specifically)  I'm wondering perhaps if I need to install some missing packages to get Xinerama working properly.

I somehow recall when installing ubuntu on a laptop of mine that it had a nice little graphical interface for doing dual screen setups... something similar to what windows has. I'm wondering if theres a way to install this and that maybe it just wasnt installed by default on my desktop PC because it isnt a laptop with a built in screen and extra VGA head for monitor attachment

In fact I've been able to get to what I think might be this same config GUI after I hit CTRL-ALT-BCKSPC and the screen flashes I get a box giving me three options.,.. continue, cancel and configure... when I hit configure I get presented with what I think is this GUI tool but in low resolution.. it lists three screens on the left side and a bunch of options for each on the right which are all set at very low resolutions and a lot of the options are actually ghosted out


I know I'm begin very verbose in explaining all this I hope I havent lost anyone anywhere.... If anyone has any ideas on what I could try to get this all working I would be truly grateful.

Thanks!!!

**So some updates from today's tinkering**

I've managed to reproduce the weird low resolution mode error... when I boot up the computer with my xorg.conf set up for dual monitor layout I get my main monitor showing up with the same low res screen and the second monitor is again a black screen with one line of white text in a command prompt style; it reads as follows

**RADEON 9250 113-DH-85010P02446 240/200 elixlie -c PAL BIOS**

also i've included the xorg.conf file that im using when i get this problem:

then a new window pops up, entitled "Monitor Resolution Settings".

This box displays icons for both of my screens, properly labelled, and shown in correct proportion to each other (one is larger than the other). It allows me to change their resolutions, and to change their positions relative to each other. However, on pressing "Apply", the screens remain cloned.

So, somehow, the system already recognises that there are two screens, but I just can't get the dual-monitor function to work.

I have no clue how to proceed to get the screens working as dual monitors.

What can I do next to diagnose, solve or resolve this problem?

### Post by Motorhead Kaze on 2008-06-26
Hello, thanks for this post. I didn't notice til now that it is from 2006...

Just an update here, in case anyone comes in using Hardy, in 2008, using an ATI graphics card...
The only thing I needed to do was add this little bit of code,in red, to my xorg.conf file. Hardy provided the ATI driver as soon as I installed it. ```

Section "Screen"
        Identifier      "Default Screen"
        Device          "Configured Video Device"
        DefaultDepth    24
       [COLOR="Red"][B] SubSection "Display"
            Depth           24
            Virtual         2560 1024
        EndSubSection[/B][/COLOR]
EndSection
```

After restarting x I went into system/preferences/screen resolution and unchecked the clone screens, pulled the monitor (white square) marked "Electronics" off to the left, selected the resolution, 1280x1024 at a refresh rate of 60hz and on the monitor to the right (another white square) marked "BenQ 19in" I selected the same resolution and refresh rate. Apply, close.

Now I have dual monitors on Ubuntu Hardy.

(Apparently, this is another issue, "...the unchecked box getting rechecked is a bug that involves a file in your home directory called ~/.gnome2/monitors.xml, which sometimes doesn't get overwitten properly. That's one reason deleting all the .g stuff worked. If the problem comes back, try deleting that file, resetting the stuff in that dialog, and restarting".)

---

Usage: lspci [<switches>]

-v		Be verbose
-n		Show numeric ID's
-nn		Show both textual and numeric ID's (names & numbers)
-b		Bus-centric view (PCI addresses and IRQ's instead of those seen by the CPU)
-x		Show hex-dump of the standard portion of config space
-xxx		Show hex-dump of the whole config space (dangerous; root only)
-xxxx		Show hex-dump of the 4096-byte extended config space (root only)
-s [[[[<domain>]:]<bus>]:][<slot>][.[<func>]]	Show only devices in selected slots
-d [<vendor>]:[<device>]	Show only selected devices
-t		Show bus tree
-m		Produce machine-readable output
-i <file>	Use specified ID database instead of /usr/share/misc/pci.ids.gz
-D		Always show domain numbers
-M		Enable `bus mapping' mode (dangerous; root only)
-P <dir>	Use specified directory instead of /proc/bus/pci
-H <mode>	Use direct hardware access (<mode> = 1 or 2)
-F <file>	Read configuration data from given file
-G		Enable PCI access debugging

### Post by Dolphin_r on 2008-06-30
> **yonasz said:**
> Can someone help me with my xorg? I have laptop 

...skipped...

After command  lspci -X | grep VGA i have this:
lspci: invalid option -- X

There is no such option "-X" You should rather use "lspci | grep -i vga"

### Post by Paddy Landau on 2008-07-07
> **kortelainen said:**
> ... theres alot of numbers, to me they are totaly random
They aren't random!

However, Xinerama is deprecated.

Do you want to try RandR? I found it way easier. See [this other post]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?p=5296990#post5296990") on where to find a how-to. Remember to back up your xorg.conf first.

---
